title: Modeling real states with union types
instruction:
A payment can be pending, paid, or failed — nothing else. A plain `string` would allow `"banana"`. A union type `"pending" | "paid" | "failed"` allows exactly those three values and nothing else.

This is one of TypeScript's most practical features.
successMessage: The `Record<PaymentStatus, string>` on line 11 ensures you handle EVERY status. If you add a new status to the union later and forget to add it to `labels`, TypeScript will immediately error — exhaustiveness checking for free.

title: Discriminated unions — the most powerful pattern
instruction:
A `Shape` can be a circle or a rectangle. Each has different fields. A discriminated union (using a `kind` or `type` field) lets TypeScript narrow the type automatically in switch statements.

This pattern eliminates entire categories of runtime errors.
successMessage: Inside each `case`, TypeScript knows the exact type. In `case "circle"`, `shape.radius` exists. In `case "rectangle"`, `shape.width` and `shape.height` exist. No casting, no runtime checks needed.

title: Intersection types — combine multiple contracts
instruction:
Intersection types (`A & B`) require a value to satisfy **both** types. This is useful for composing reusable type pieces — like mixing audit trail fields into any entity.

Build an auditable entity system.
successMessage: Intersection types compose perfectly. `WithAudit` and `WithSoftDelete` are reusable type mixins — add them to `UserEntity`, `OrderEntity`, or any other entity with a single `&`.
